html:
Arrow direction
Demo steps
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: five identical pinhole camera frusta (wireframe pyramids with square base), all oriented in the same direction
five black dots representing the camera center for each frustum
four thick red horizontal arrows pointing along the positive X direction, connecting each camera center to the next
ground/horizontal reference plane for spatial context (optional, depending on design)
spatial axis annotations (X, Y, Z) at one corner for reference

interactions.label: Camera steps
interactions.teaches: Shows the camera's lateral path with a variable number of discrete positions, emphasizing the translation trajectory

interactions.label: Lateral distance
interactions.teaches: Adjusts the distance between each camera position to demonstrate how the translation magnitude affects observed motion

interactions.label: Arrow direction
interactions.teaches: Switches between viewing red arrows as indicating the camera's real motion or (when reversed) the resultant apparent scene flow direction

demo_steps.title: Camera starts at first pose
demo_steps.narration: Here is a pinhole camera at its initial position, with a thick red arrow indicating the direction of lateral movement. The orientation of the camera does not change; only its center moves sideways.

demo_steps.title: Multiple lateral views
demo_steps.narration: Now we show five evenly spaced camera positions along the lateral trajectory, each connected by a red arrow. This sequence represents how a camera moves sideways, just like looking through a car window as you drive past a scene.

demo_steps.title: Apparent scene motion is opposite
demo_steps.narration: If we interpret the arrows as the apparent motion of objects in the scene (as projected in the image), their direction reverses. When the camera moves right, everything in the image appears to drift left. This illustrates how motion in the image is opposite the camera's translation.

demo_steps.title: Changing lateral velocity
demo_steps.narration: Let's increase the lateral travel per step. The cameras are now spaced farther apart, which would cause closer objects to appear to move faster across the image plane — this demonstrates the relationship between camera speed and perceived motion.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Slightly elevated, angled perspective showing the row of camera centers extending left to right across the screen. Enough zoom out to fit all positions and arrows horizontally.
interactionPlan.notes: Each camera frustum should be rendered with light wireframe so arrows and dots are clearly visible. When numSteps or stepSize changes, recalculate all camera positions and arrows accordingly. For the arrowDirection toggle, display large, animated arrows and smoothly reverse their orientation. If ground plane is included, downplay its contrast to keep focus on cameras and arrows.

Arrow direction
Demo steps
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: No content rendered: frustum shapes, arrows, or any scene elements missing entirely.
evaluation.notes: Fatal JavaScript error prevents any figure from rendering, so all primitives, interactions, and labels are missing.
action_items: Fix the JavaScript syntax error causing failure to render any content.
Test for valid HTML/JS output before submission; ensure no document.write or syntax issues.
Verify that the intended frusta, camera dots, translation arrows, and labels appear and are interactive as described.
Check that red arrows and camera elements match style, count, and arrangement from the reference image.

feedback.rationale: The plan is conceptually correct but a fatal JavaScript error prevented any rendering, so the implementation needs to be fixed.

discrepancies: No rendered figure elements are visible in the screenshot due to a startup error.
None of the camera frustums, red arrows, or arrangement from the source are present.
No axes, grid, or labeled points are visible.
No arrows or colored primitive elements are present, despite them being prominent in the source.
evaluation.notes: Code fails to render any figure due to a syntax error, making the entire output unrecognizable and unusable.
action_items: Fix the initial syntax or token error so that the figure can render at all.
Check for typos or browser-incompatible constructs in the HTML/JS blending.
Once fixed, verify that geometry, red arrows, and camera objects match the layout and count of the source figure.
Remove all extraneous 3D elements (like axes/grid) not present in the original unless specifically pedagogical.

feedback.rationale: The primary issue is a code syntax/startup error preventing rendering; the concept and plan are not flagged as inaccurate or incomplete.

Arrow direction
Demo steps
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: No rendered figure or scene is present due to a fatal error.
None of the camera/frustum shapes or red arrows from the source figure appear in the output.
No black dots, geometric structures, or repeated array is visible.
No color or proportional elements rendered.
No labels or arrows present visually.
evaluation.notes: The code fails to run and displays only a syntax error, resulting in no figure or interactions.
action_items: Fix the fatal syntax error so the code executes and produces output.
Ensure all camera/frustum shapes and red arrows with the correct count, arrangement, and color are visible as in the reference.
Add and position all labels appropriately beneath each object and along the axes, matching source clarity.
Review material settings, camera, and proportions to accurately reflect the source's simple 2D look.
Test interactivity after corrections to ensure UI controls impact the displayed figure as intended.

feedback.rationale: The failure is due to a fatal syntax error and missing rendered elements, but the action items and issues indicate a generation (rendering/implementation) breakdown, not a conceptual planning flaw.
